ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'tea beverage' class (FOODON_03301099); no specific class exists for honey-sweetened oolong tea. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers oolong tea as a base; honey sweetening and no-topping variant are not separately coded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227432005 covers tea drink as a substance class; honey-sweetened oolong variant is not separately coded. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (250 g), equivalent to one takeaway cup of honey oolong tea without toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Oolong Tea — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D08010059 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: Honey, GI value from International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for honey oolong tea as a beverage in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 based on honey GI values (range 35–58, central estimate 55) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), which is the dominant glycaemic driver in this beverage. A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55) per the University of Sydney GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Oolong tea base contributes negligible carbohydrate. GL per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (7.49 g/100 g × 250 g serving = 18.73 g) / 100 = 10.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.49 g sugar per 100 g; liquid sugar independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance, despite very low sodium (1 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this beverage due to its sugar-sweetened beverage classification (7.49 g sugar/100 g), as added sugar intake independently elevates blood pressure risk; unsweetened oolong tea is the recommended alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.49 g sugar per 100 g and 0 g dietary fibre; honey in liquid form causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation with no fibre buffer.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage as it is a sugar-sweetened drink delivering 7.49 g of rapidly absorbed honey-derived sugar per 100 g with zero dietary fibre, causing rapid postprandial glucose spikes; unsweetened oolong tea is the clinically preferred alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk; this beverage contains 7.49 g sugar/100 g with negligible fat and very low sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this beverage due to its sugar-sweetened beverage classification; while fat and sodium are negligible, habitual added-sugar intake from liquid sources is independently associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory sugar load of 7.49 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre outweighs any diluted polyphenol contribution from oolong tea at beverage concentration.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as the added sugar burden promotes systemic inflammation; unsweetened oolong tea would provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols without the sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Oolong Tea (No Topping) is a freshly brewed oolong tea sweetened with honey, served without any customisable toppings such as pearls, jelly, or pudding. It is a popular ready-to-drink speciality tea beverage widely available at bubble tea and speciality tea shops across Singapore. The beverage is prepared by brewing oolong tea leaves and blending the infusion with honey to achieve a floral, lightly sweet flavour profile with the characteristic roasted and orchid-like notes of oolong tea. The absence of toppings means the drink is purely liquid with no added texture components. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 29 kcal, 7.49 g carbohydrate (entirely as sugar from honey), 0.01 g protein, 0 g fat, 0 g dietary fibre, and 1 mg sodium. The entire carbohydrate content is derived from honey, making this a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) by clinical classification. Despite the low calorie count per 100 g, a standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 18.7 g of sugar and a glycaemic load of around 10.3, which is clinically significant for blood glucose management. The beverage is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension due to its SSB classification. Oolong tea polyphenols may offer antioxidant benefits in unsweetened form, but at the diluted concentrations present in a sweetened commercial beverage, the anti-inflammatory benefit is outweighed by the pro-inflammatory sugar load. The drink is vegetarian, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010059, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). This is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. Source match type is exact.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database for this beverage formulation; honey oolong tea contains no fat by product composition. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by the same source. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as brewed tea with honey contains no dietary fibre. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this specific honey oolong tea beverage is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on honey GI values (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) as no direct GI measurement for honey oolong tea exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI ≤55 is classified as low; a GI of 55 therefore falls in the low category at its upper boundary. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to align with the University of Sydney GI classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). GL per 250 g serving = 55 × 18.73 g / 100 = 10.3. Calcium reported as 1 mg/100 g and iron as 0.01 mg/100 g per HPB source.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields (foodon_match_type, foodex2_match_type, snomed_ct_match_type) from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to align with VERITAS_SCORING_MODEL_v2.md requirements for g5_ontology_honesty.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index of 55. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI ≤55 is classified as low; GI 56–69 is medium; GI ≥70 is high. A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category. Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this correction.
